questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes IoC, DI, and DIP?
options: IoC is the broad concept, DI is one implementation of it, and DIP is the SOLID principle that DI fulfils
DIP is the broad concept, IoC is its principle, and DI is the SOLID design rule
DI is the broad concept, IoC is one implementation of it, and DIP is unrelated to either
IoC, DI, and DIP are three interchangeable names for the same container mechanism
questions.explanation: IoC is the concept (the framework controls flow/creation), DI is one way to achieve IoC, and DIP is the SOLID principle ("depend on abstractions") that DI serves at runtime. The other options scramble these roles or wrongly equate them.

questions.question: How many instances does a **Transient** service produce when it is resolved twice within a single HTTP request?
options: Two — a Transient gives a fresh instance on every single resolve, even twice in one request
One — Transient still shares a single instance per request like Scoped
One — the container caches Transient instances for the life of the request
Zero — Transient services are only created lazily when the request completes
questions.explanation: Transient means a new instance on every resolve, so two resolves in one request yield two distinct instances. Sharing one instance per request describes Scoped, not Transient.

questions.question: What lifetime does `AddDbContext<AppDbContext>()` register the `DbContext` with by default, and why is that the correct choice?
options: Scoped — one `DbContext` per request matches the unit-of-work model and avoids sharing a non-thread-safe context
Singleton — one shared `DbContext` minimizes connection overhead across the app
Transient — a brand-new `DbContext` per resolve guarantees the change tracker is always empty
Scoped, but only because EF Core forbids any other lifetime from compiling
questions.explanation: `AddDbContext` registers the context as Scoped, giving one instance per request, which fits the unit-of-work model and avoids sharing the non-thread-safe context across threads. Singleton would corrupt the change tracker, and EF Core does not forbid other lifetimes at compile time.

questions.question: ASP.NET Core's built-in DI container supports property injection out of the box, the same way it supports constructor injection.
questions.explanation: Constructor injection is first-class in the built-in container; property injection is not supported out of the box. The reviewer lists property injection as a code smell that the built-in container does not perform automatically.

questions.question: When resolving a service manually from an `IServiceProvider`, why does the reviewer prefer `GetRequiredService<T>()` over `GetService<T>()`?
options: `GetRequiredService<T>()` throws if `T` isn't registered, so a missing registration fails loudly instead of returning `null`
`GetRequiredService<T>()` is faster because it skips the container's lifetime checks
`GetService<T>()` always creates a new Transient instance, breaking Singleton semantics
`GetRequiredService<T>()` automatically registers `T` as Scoped if it is missing
questions.explanation: `GetRequiredService` throws when the type isn't registered, surfacing the bug immediately, whereas `GetService` silently returns null. It does not change performance, lifetime semantics, or auto-register anything.

questions.question: A `Singleton` service takes a `Scoped` service as a constructor parameter, in a default Development host. What happens?
options: It throws at `app.Build()` because both `ValidateScopes` and `ValidateOnBuild` are on in Development
It works fine; the singleton simply gets a fresh scoped instance on each request
It throws a compile-time error because the lifetimes are incompatible
It silently downgrades the singleton to a scoped lifetime to resolve the conflict
questions.explanation: In Development both `ValidateScopes` and `ValidateOnBuild` are enabled, so the captive dependency fails fast at `app.Build()` with "Cannot consume scoped service ... from singleton ...". Lifetimes are not validated at compile time, and the container never silently downgrades a lifetime.

questions.question: A singleton background processor legitimately needs to use a `Scoped` repository. What is the correct pattern?
options: Inject `IServiceScopeFactory`, create a scope per operation, and resolve the scoped service inside that scope
Inject the scoped repository directly and reuse the same instance for the app lifetime
Re-register the repository as a Singleton so the lifetimes match
Call `services.BuildServiceProvider()` inside the processor each time you need the repository
questions.explanation: The safe pattern is to inject `IServiceScopeFactory`, create a scope per unit of work, and resolve the scoped service inside it. Injecting it directly captures it forever, re-registering as Singleton makes a non-thread-safe service shared, and calling `BuildServiceProvider()` builds a duplicate container (ASP0000).

questions.question: Given the registrations below, what does a constructor parameter of type `INotificationChannel` (a single, non-enumerable resolution) receive?
options: A `PushChannel` — a single resolution returns the **last** registered implementation
An `EmailChannel` — a single resolution returns the **first** registered implementation
All three, wrapped automatically into a composite channel
It throws because the same interface was registered more than once
questions.explanation: A single (non-enumerable) resolution returns the last-registered implementation, here `PushChannel`. Resolving `IEnumerable<INotificationChannel>` would return all three in registration order; the container does not throw on duplicate registrations.

questions.question: Which .NET 8 feature lets you register multiple implementations of one interface under distinct keys and resolve a specific one at the injection site without a hand-rolled factory?
options: Keyed services, resolved with `[FromKeyedServices("key")]` or `GetRequiredKeyedService<T>(key)`
`IEnumerable<T>` injection, which returns the implementation matching a string key
`TryAddEnumerable`, which de-duplicates implementations by their key
`IOptionsSnapshot<T>`, which exposes a named variant per request
questions.explanation: Keyed services (added in .NET 8) register implementations under keys and resolve them with `[FromKeyedServices]` or `GetRequiredKeyedService<T>`. `IEnumerable<T>` returns all implementations (no key matching), `TryAddEnumerable` de-dupes by implementation type, and `IOptionsSnapshot` is the options pattern, not service keying.

questions.question: A **scoped** service must observe configuration changes that are reloaded at runtime, picking up the latest values per request. Which options accessor should it inject?
options: `IOptionsSnapshot<T>` — scoped, recomputed per request, reflecting config reloads
`IOptions<T>` — a singleton snapshot bound once at startup
`IOptionsMonitor<T>` — but only when injected into a Transient service
`IConfiguration` read directly with magic-string keys per request
questions.explanation: `IOptionsSnapshot<T>` is scoped and recomputed per request, so it reflects config reloads for scoped consumers. `IOptions<T>` is a singleton bound once; `IOptionsMonitor<T>` suits singletons/background services needing live change notifications; reading `IConfiguration` directly is exactly the magic-string approach the options pattern replaces.

questions.question: In which scenarios does the reviewer recommend `AddDbContextFactory` instead of the default `AddDbContext`? (Select all that apply.)
options: Blazor Server, where a circuit/component can outlive a request and concurrent renders may overlap
Singletons or background services that need a `DbContext` outside a request scope
Standard request-scoped controller actions in a typical ASP.NET Core API
Anywhere you want short, explicitly-owned contexts you dispose deterministically
questions.explanation: The factory fits Blazor Server, singletons/background services, and any place you want short, explicitly-owned contexts you dispose yourself. Standard request-scoped controller actions are the normal case where `AddDbContext` (Scoped) is correct.

questions.question: Why does the reviewer warn against calling `services.BuildServiceProvider()` yourself inside `Program.cs` to grab a service during registration?
options: It builds a second, separate container with duplicate singletons and no scope validation (analyzer warning ASP0000)
It permanently disables constructor injection for the rest of the app
It is only legal in non-Development environments and throws in Development
It forces every subsequent registration to become Transient
questions.explanation: Calling `BuildServiceProvider()` during registration creates a second container with duplicate singletons and no scope validation, which the ASP0000 analyzer flags. The recommended fix is the factory overload that hands you the `IServiceProvider`. It does not disable injection, throw by environment, or change lifetimes.

questions.question: A `Transient` service injected into a `Singleton` constructor effectively lives as long as the singleton, even though it was registered as "new every time."
questions.explanation: Because the singleton is constructed once and holds the reference forever, the captured Transient lives for the singleton's lifetime — it effectively becomes a singleton, defeating the "new every time" expectation.

questions.question: What is the most common accidental memory leak the reviewer describes around `IDisposable` services?
options: A Transient `IDisposable` resolved from the **root** provider — its disposal is deferred until shutdown, so they accumulate for the app's lifetime
A Scoped `IDisposable` — the container never disposes it, so each request leaks one instance
A Singleton `IDisposable` — it is disposed on every request, causing use-after-dispose bugs
Any `IDisposable` resolved inside a request scope — the container forgets to track it
questions.explanation: Transient disposables resolved from the root provider have disposal deferred until application shutdown, so they accumulate as an effective leak. Scoped and properly scope-resolved transients are disposed at scope end, and singletons are disposed at shutdown — not per request.

questions.question: Which statements about who disposes services are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: The container disposes Scoped disposables when the request scope ends
The container disposes Singleton disposables when the application (root container) shuts down
The container disposes an instance you supplied via `AddSingleton(new MyService())`
The container disposes Transient disposables it created, tracked by the scope that resolved them
questions.explanation: The container owns disposal of the instances it creates: Scoped at scope end, Singleton at shutdown, and container-created Transients tracked by the resolving scope. It does NOT dispose an instance you constructed and registered yourself via `AddSingleton(new T())` — you own that object's lifetime.

questions.question: Why does the reviewer recommend explicitly enabling `ValidateScopes` in **all** environments rather than relying on the defaults?
options: In non-Development environments scope validation is off by default, so a captive dependency is created silently and ships as a latent bug
In all environments scope validation is off by default, so captive dependencies are never caught automatically
Enabling it in Production makes singletons thread-safe automatically
It is required for keyed services to resolve correctly in Production
questions.explanation: Scope validation is on by default only in Development; in Production and other non-Development environments it is off, so a captive dependency slips through silently. Forcing `ValidateScopes` on everywhere catches it. It does not make singletons thread-safe and is unrelated to keyed-service resolution.

questions.question: A host has `ValidateScopes` enabled but `ValidateOnBuild` disabled. A singleton captures a scoped service. When and how does the failure surface?
options: A bare `InvalidOperationException` with "Cannot consume scoped service ... from singleton ..." at resolve time, not at build
An `AggregateException` thrown at `app.Build()` during startup
No exception at all — `ValidateScopes` alone never detects captive dependencies
A compile-time error, because `ValidateScopes` runs the analyzer at build
questions.explanation: With only `ValidateScopes` on (no `ValidateOnBuild`), the captive dependency is caught when the graph is resolved, surfacing as a bare `InvalidOperationException` with that exact message. The `AggregateException` at `app.Build()` requires `ValidateOnBuild`; `ValidateScopes` does detect the problem and is a runtime check, not a compile-time analyzer.

questions.question: A library extension method wants to register a default `INotificationChannel` only if the host hasn't already supplied that same implementation, while still allowing other channels to coexist. Which API matches that intent?
options: `services.TryAddEnumerable(ServiceDescriptor.Scoped<INotificationChannel, EmailChannel>())` — adds to the multi-registration set, de-duped by implementation type
`services.TryAddScoped<INotificationChannel, EmailChannel>()` — registers only if no `INotificationChannel` of any kind exists yet
`services.AddScoped<INotificationChannel, EmailChannel>()` — always adds, even if `EmailChannel` is already in the set
`services.AddKeyedScoped<INotificationChannel, EmailChannel>("email")` — keying it prevents any duplicate registration
questions.explanation: `TryAddEnumerable` adds to the multi-registration set but de-duplicates by implementation type, which is exactly "register my default unless the host already added this implementation" while letting other channels coexist. `TryAddScoped` would skip if any `INotificationChannel` exists, plain `AddScoped` always adds a duplicate, and keying changes the resolution model rather than providing de-duped defaults.
